title: Raising Exceptions 🧨
description: Take control and intentionally trigger your own errors.

slides.title: Intentional Sabotage 🧨
slides.content:
Why would you ever want to forcefully crash your own program manually?

If you are writing a banking application, and a user tries to withdraw a negative amount of money, you absolutely DO NOT want the program to silently process that negative number down the line!

You must aggressively stop execution right there and violently alert other developers.

slides.title: The `raise` Keyword
slides.content: Run this! We intentionally instantiate a brand new `ArgumentError` object and forcefully violently throw it using the `raise` keyword to kill execution and protect our bank.
slides.codeSnippet:
def withdraw(amount)
  if amount < 0
    # Throw an intentional bomb to violently kill the app
    raise ArgumentError.new("Negative amounts are illegal!")
  end
  
  puts "Withdrawing $#{amount}"
end

withdraw(-50)

quizData.question: Which explicit keyword deliberately throws an Exception object into the system to crash the program or trigger a rescue block?
quizData.explanation: You `raise` the alarm precisely when your data logic catches something incredibly illegal happening.

slides.title: Raising Shorthand
slides.content:
By default, if you don't explicitly explicitly instantiate a specific class like `ArgumentError.new("msg")`, and just type `raise "msg"`, Ruby automatically generates a generic `RuntimeError` object and throws that instead.

This is widely used for incredibly fast, simple error throwing!

quizData.question: If you brutally type `raise "I am dying!"`, what type of Exception Class does Ruby automatically generate secretly in the background?
quizData.explanation: Supplying just a naked string intelligently defaults strictly to raising a pure `RuntimeError` exception object containing that exact string message.
